auth.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog auth` to access this command group. Run `--help` on a subcommand for details.

login.help: Configure authentication credentials. Prompts for required credentials based on the tool's authentication type. For OAuth authorization code flows, opens a browser for user consent.
login.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog auth login` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --profile
options.help: Profile name to save credentials to

options.name: --force
options.help: Clear existing ephemeral auth state and re-authenticate

logout.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog auth logout` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --profile
options.help: Profile name to clear credentials from

delete.help: Delete a profile and its data.

arguments.help: Profile name to get details for

options.name: --limit
options.help: Maximum number of profiles to return

options.name: --properties
options.help: Comma-separated list of fields to include

remove.help: Delete a profile and its data.

rename.help: Rename a profile, re-keying its secrets to the new profile name.

arguments.name: new
arguments.help: New profile name (must not already exist)

options.name: --keep-old
options.help: Build and activate the new profile but leave the old profile dir and old secrets intact

select.help: Activate a profile within its auth type.

arguments.name: name
arguments.help: Profile name to activate within its auth type

status.help: Check authentication status across profiles. Performs a live round-trip for every configured credential type so the report reflects ground truth — not on-disk belief. Saved credentials whose live verification fails are reported as ``authenticated: false`` with the failure reason in ``api_test``.
status.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog auth status` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --profile
options.help: Profile name to check (defaults to all profiles)

test.help: Test authentication by verifying credentials work across profiles.
test.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog auth test` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

comments.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page comments` to access this command group. Run `--help` on a subcommand for details.

add.help: Add a comment to a Notion page. Posts a top-level comment to the specified page using the same Notion authentication as the rest of the `notion-page` subcommands.
examples: ata-blog notion-page comments add PAGE_ID --body "No affiliate match — needs human review"
ata-blog notion-page comments add PAGE_ID -b "Quick note from CLI"
add.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page comments add` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

get.help: Get a single Notion comment by ID.
get.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page comments get` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

list.help: List comments on a Notion page with context. Returns comments with the text they're attached to, useful for gathering human review feedback from Notion inline comments.
list.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page comments list` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

set.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page content set` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --file
options.help: Markdown file to set as content

create.help: Create a new article idea in the Notion database. Creates a page using the Standard ATA Tutorial AI-Created Idea template with the provided title, excerpt, category, and optional keywords.
examples: ata-blog notion-page create --title "My Post" --excerpt "Desc" --category "Cloud" --keywords "azure, cloud" --status "Good Idea"
create.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page create` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --status
options.help: Override default status (template sets 'Idea' by default)

options.name: --template-id
options.help: Notion template ID (defaults to Standard ATA Tutorial AI-Created Idea)

schema.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page schema` to access this command group. Run `--help` on a subcommand for details.

add-property.help: Add a new property (column) to a Notion database schema. Delegates to the underlying `notion field add` CLI, which performs the PATCH https://api.notion.com/v1/databases/{database_id} call using the same Notion authentication path as every other ata-blog notion-page subcommand. On success, emits a JSON object to stdout: {"database_id": "...", "property_name": "...", "type": "...", "created": true}
examples: ata-blog notion-page schema add-property DB_ID --name "Affiliate Promotion" --type rich_text
ata-blog notion-page schema add-property DB_ID --name "Source URL" --type url
ata-blog notion-page schema add-property DB_ID --name "Reviewed" --type checkbox
add-property.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page schema add-property` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --type
options.help: Property type. Supported (empty-config types only): rich_text, url, checkbox, number, date, email, phone_number, files. (select/multi_select require options and are out of scope for this command.)

search.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page search` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

statuses.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog notion-page statuses` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

unpublish.help: Unpublish an article: revert a published post back to draft.
unpublish.usage_instructions: Inverse of `publish`. The identifier may be a Notion page ID (32-hex, dashed or not), a WordPress post ID, a post URL, or a slug; the command resolves BOTH the Notion page and the WordPress post. By default the WordPress post is trashed (recoverable); pass --force to permanently delete it, or --keep-wordpress to leave WordPress untouched. The Notion page is reset to 'Draft' (override with --status, validated against live statuses) and its publication artifact fields are cleared (Published URL, X Post URL, LinkedIn Post URL, Publish Date -> null; Promoted -> false); content, Keywords, Tags, Category, Schema Type, Stage Date, and Post Performance Snapshots are left intact. This is destructive: pass --yes to run non-interactively or --dry-run to preview without mutating. Output is a JSON summary of the WordPress action and the Notion fields cleared.

arguments.name: identifier
arguments.help: Notion page ID, WordPress post ID, post URL, or slug

options.name: --force
options.help: Permanently delete the WordPress post (vs trash)

options.name: --keep-wordpress
options.help: Skip the WordPress trash/delete; only reset Notion

options.name: --dry-run
options.help: Resolve and report planned changes without mutating

examples: ata-blog notion-page update PAGE_ID -s "Draft" -p "Dev Review Iterations:2"
update.usage_instructions: Property types are read from the live Notion database schema. Pass an empty value (e.g. -p "Published URL:") to clear a property: url/email/number/select/date become null and rich_text/multi_select/relation become empty. Checkbox properties accept true/false/yes/no/1/0 (case-insensitive) and are rejected if ambiguous.

raptive.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog raptive` to access this command group. Run `--help` on a subcommand for details.

disable.help: Disable Raptive ads on a WordPress post. By default disables ALL ads. Use flags to disable specific ad types only.
examples: ata-blog raptive disable 12345                    # Disable all ads
ata-blog raptive disable 12345 --content-only     # Disable content ads only
ata-blog raptive disable 12345 --video-only       # Disable video players only
ata-blog raptive disable 12345 --re-enable-days 30  # Re-enable after 30 days
disable.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog raptive disable` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --preview-only
options.help: Disable only ads shown while previewing in WordPress admin

options.name: --type
options.help: WordPress resource type: post or page

enable.help: Re-enable Raptive ads on a WordPress post. Removes the disable flags from post meta to restore ads.
examples: ata-blog raptive enable 12345                  # Enable all ads
ata-blog raptive enable 12345 --content-only  # Enable content ads only
ata-blog raptive enable 12345 --video-only    # Enable video players only
enable.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog raptive enable` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --preview-only
options.help: Enable only ads shown while previewing in WordPress admin

options.name: --type
options.help: WordPress resource type: post or page

fields.help: List all Raptive post meta field names. Useful for manual debugging or direct API calls.
fields.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog raptive fields` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

status.help: Check Raptive ad status for a WordPress post. Shows which ad types are disabled and any scheduled re-enable date.
status.usage_instructions: Use `ata-blog raptive status` with the documented arguments and options shown below.

options.name: --type
options.help: WordPress resource type: post or page
